subject: Physics
q: An amount of ice of mass $10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$ and temperature $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is transformed to vapour of temperature $110^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ by applying heat. The total amount of work required for this conversion is, (Take, specific heat of ice $=2100 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$, specific heat of water $=4180 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$, specific heat of steam $=1920 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$, Latent heat of ice $=3.35 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1}$ and Latent heat of steam $=2.25 \times 10^6$ $\mathrm{Jkg}^{-1}$ )
question: An amount of ice of mass $10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$ and temperature $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is transformed to vapour of temperature $110^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ by applying heat. The total amount of work required for this conversion is, (Take, specific heat of ice $=2100 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$, specific heat of water $=4180 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$, specific heat of steam $=1920 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$, Latent heat of ice $=3.35 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1}$ and Latent heat of steam $=2.25 \times 10^6$ $\mathrm{Jkg}^{-1}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{Q}_1=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{S}_1 \times \Delta \mathrm{T}=10^{-3} \times 2100 \times 10=21 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{Q}_2=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{f}}=10^{-3} \times 3.35 \times 10^5=335 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{Q}_3=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{w}} \times \Delta \mathrm{T}=10^{-3} \times 4180 \times 100=418 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{Q}_4=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{v}}=10^{-3} \times 2.25 \times 10^6=2250 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{Q}_5=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{v}} \times \Delta \mathrm{T}=10^{-3} \times 1920 \times 10=19.2 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{net}}=3043.2 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A pendulum clock loses 12 s a day if the temperature is <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> C and gains 4s a day if the temperature is <math><msup><mrow><mn>20</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> C. The temperature at which the clock will show correct time, and the co-efficient of linear expansion <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> of the metal of the pendulum shaft are respectively:
question: A pendulum clock loses 12 s a day if the temperature is <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> C and gains 4s a day if the temperature is <math><msup><mrow><mn>20</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> C. The temperature at which the clock will show correct time, and the co-efficient of linear expansion <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> of the metal of the pendulum shaft are respectively:
solution:
∆TT=12∆ll=12α∆θ 

124=40-θθ-20

3θ-60=40-θ

4θ=100

θ=25oC

Also,∆T=12α ∆θ×T

4=12α 5×86400 

85×86400=α 

α=1.85×10-5  /oC

subject: Physics
q: The resistances of the platinum wire of a platinum resistance thermometer at the ice point and steam point are $8 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$ respectively. After inserting in a hot bath of temperature $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the resistance of platinum wire is :
question: The resistances of the platinum wire of a platinum resistance thermometer at the ice point and steam point are $8 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$ respectively. After inserting in a hot bath of temperature $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the resistance of platinum wire is :
solution:
Given $R_0=8 \Omega, R_{100}=10 \Omega$ $\therefore \mathrm{R}_{100}=\mathrm{R}_0(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T})$
Also, $R_{400}=R_0\left(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}^1\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & \therefore 10=8(1+\alpha \times 100) \Rightarrow 100 \alpha=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{R}_{400}=8(1+400 \alpha)=8(1+1)=16 \Omega \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A liquid in a beaker has temperature $\theta(t)$ at time $t$ and $\theta_0$ is temperature of surroundings, then according to Newton's law of cooling the correct graph between $\log _e\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$ and $t$ is
question: A liquid in a beaker has temperature $\theta(t)$ at time $t$ and $\theta_0$ is temperature of surroundings, then according to Newton's law of cooling the correct graph between $\log _e\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$ and $t$ is
solution:
According to Newtons law of cooling.
$\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto-\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$
$\int \frac{d \theta}{\theta-\theta_0}=\int-k d t$
$\Rightarrow \quad \ln \left(\theta-\theta_0\right)=-k t+c$
Hence the plot of $\ln \left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$ vs $t$ should be a straight line with negative slope.

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is converted into steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> by boiling at atmospheric pressure. The volume of water changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> as a liquid to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>671</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> as steam. The change in internal energy of the system during the process will be (Given latent heat of vaporisation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2257</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, Atmospheric pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is converted into steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> by boiling at atmospheric pressure. The volume of water changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> as a liquid to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>671</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> as steam. The change in internal energy of the system during the process will be (Given latent heat of vaporisation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2257</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, Atmospheric pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
The work to be done in the process is given by

dW=PdV=1×105 Pa×(1.671-0.001) m3=1.670×105 J

The change in heat energy during the vaporisation process can be calculated as follows-

ΔQsupplied =2257×1×103 J=22.57×105 J

Hence, the change in internal energy in the process is given by

ΔU=ΔQsupplied-ΔW=(22.57-1.67)×105 J=20.9×105 J=2090 kJ

q: As per the given figure, two plates A and B of thermal conductivity K and 2K are joined together to form a compound plate. The thickness of plates are 4.0 cm and 2.5 cm respectively and the area of cross-section is 120 cm2 for each plate. The equivalent thermal conductivity of the compound plate is 1+5αK, then the value of α will be _____ .
question: As per the given figure, two plates A and B of thermal conductivity K and 2K are joined together to form a compound plate. The thickness of plates are 4.0 cm and 2.5 cm respectively and the area of cross-section is 120 cm2 for each plate. The equivalent thermal conductivity of the compound plate is 1+5αK, then the value of α will be _____ .
solution:
The thermal resistance of rod 1 will be R1=l1KA=4KA and rod 2 will be R2=l22KA=2.52KA.

Since they are connected in series, Req=R1+R2=10.52KA.

If equivalent thermal conductivity is Keq, then Req=l1+l2KeqA=6.5KeqA. Therefore,

10.52KA=6.5KeqA⇒Keq=2621K=1+521K

Hence, α=21.

question: A calorimeter of water equivalent <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><none></none><mo>'</mo><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>'</mo></mmultiscripts></math>' grams of steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is mixed in it till the temperature of the mixture is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>31</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><none></none><mo>'</mo><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>'</mo></mmultiscripts></math> is close to (Latent heat of water<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mn>540</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, specific heat of water<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>°</mo></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
20031-25=m×540+m169

1200=m609

m≈2

subject: Physics
q: Resistance of the wire is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> respectively. Temperature coefficient of resistance of the material of the wire is
question: Resistance of the wire is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> respectively. Temperature coefficient of resistance of the material of the wire is
solution:
Resistance of a material is related to temperature by expression R=R01+αΔT

Here, at 10°C, we have  2=R01+10α   ...i

And at 30°C, we have 3=R01+30α    ...ii

From equation i, R0=21+10α 

Putting this value in equation ii, 

3=21+10α1+30α⇒3+30α=2+60α

⇒1=30α

Hence, temperature coefficient of the material of wire is α=130=0.033°C-1

q: An external pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is applied on a cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> so that it is equally compressed from all sides. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is the bulk modulus of the material of the cube and <math><mi>α</mi></math> is its coefficient of linear expansion. Suppose we want to bring the cube to its original size by heating. The temperature should be raised by:
question: An external pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is applied on a cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> so that it is equally compressed from all sides. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is the bulk modulus of the material of the cube and <math><mi>α</mi></math> is its coefficient of linear expansion. Suppose we want to bring the cube to its original size by heating. The temperature should be raised by:
solution:
B=-PdVV

dVV=-PB

dV=-PVB

ΔV=VγΔTΔVV=γΔT

ΔVV=3αΔT

K=PΔVV=P3αΔT

ΔT=P3αk

subject: Physics
q: Two different wires having lengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and respective temperature coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> are joined end-to-end. Then the effective temperature coefficient of linear expansion is :
question: Two different wires having lengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and respective temperature coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> are joined end-to-end. Then the effective temperature coefficient of linear expansion is :
solution:
The wire are joined end-to-end, so they are in series combination, then total length of wire is given by
L=L1+L2  ...(1)

When the temperature is increase by ∆θ, then its final length is given by using concept of linear expansion,
L'=L(1+α∆θ)  ...(2)

Using equation (1) and (2) for the series combination of wires, final length is given by

L'=L1'+L2'  ...(3)

⇒L1+L2(1+αeff∆θ)=L1(1+α1∆θ)+L2(1+α2∆θ)

⇒αeff=L1α1+L2α2L1+L2

q: Rods $x$ and $y$ of equal dimensions but of different materials are joined as shown in figure. Temperatures of end points $A$ and $F$ are maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Given the thermal conductivity of $\operatorname{rod} x$ is three times of that of $\operatorname{rod} y$, the temperature at junction points $B$ and $E$ are (close to):
question: Rods $x$ and $y$ of equal dimensions but of different materials are joined as shown in figure. Temperatures of end points $A$ and $F$ are maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Given the thermal conductivity of $\operatorname{rod} x$ is three times of that of $\operatorname{rod} y$, the temperature at junction points $B$ and $E$ are (close to):
solution:
Let $\left[ R = \dfrac{\ell}{3KA} \right]$

$\left[ H = \dfrac{100 - 40}{\dfrac{11R}{2}} \right]$ ...(1)
$H = \dfrac{100 - T_B}{R}$ .....(2)
$H = \dfrac{T_E - 40}{3R}$ .....(3)
using (1) and (2)
$120 = 1100 - 11T_A$
$T_B = 89°C$
using (1) and (3)
$T_E = 73°C$

q: Two identical beakers <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> contain equal volumes of two different liquids at <math><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> each and left to cool down. Liquid in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> has density of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and specific heat of <math><mn>2000</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> while the liquid in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> has density <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and specific heat of <math><mn>4000</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Which of the following best describes their temperature versus time graph schematically? (assume the emissivity of both the beakers to be the same)
question: Two identical beakers <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> contain equal volumes of two different liquids at <math><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> each and left to cool down. Liquid in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> has density of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and specific heat of <math><mn>2000</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> while the liquid in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> has density <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and specific heat of <math><mn>4000</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Which of the following best describes their temperature versus time graph schematically? (assume the emissivity of both the beakers to be the same)
solution:
From Newton's law of cooling,
Rate of cooling: -dTdt=4σeAT03T-T0ms ∝1ms  
∴ρA<ρB
mA<mB
And sA<sB
mAsA<mBsB
At  t=0,-dTdtA>-dTdtB
Initially, the rate of cooling for A will be higher than B and temperature (T)  vs time (t) graph will be a curve.

subject: Physics
q: A body cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes. If, temperature of surroundings is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Then, after the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes, its temperature will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
question: A body cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes. If, temperature of surroundings is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Then, after the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes, its temperature will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
solution:
From Newton’s law of cooling

dTdt=kTavg-T0, here, T0 is surrounding temperature.

For case 1:

60-406=k60+402-10

206=k50-10    ...1 

For case 2:

40-T6=k40+T2-10    ...2 

From equation 1, we have k=112

Putting in equation 2

40-T6=11240+T2-10

⇒10+T2=80-2T⇒2T+T2=70

⇒T=1405=28°C

subject: Physics
q: In 5 minutes, a body cools from 75 °C to 65 °C at room temperature of 25 °C. The temperature of body at the end of next 5 minutes is           C∘.
question: In 5 minutes, a body cools from 75 °C to 65 °C at room temperature of 25 °C. The temperature of body at the end of next 5 minutes is           C∘.
solution:
By newton's law of cooling (with approximation), 
ΔTΔt=-CTag-Ts

1st -10 °C5 min=-C70 °C-25 °C

⇒C=245 min-1

2nd T-655 min=-CT+652-25=-245T+152

⇒9( T-65)=-(T+15)
⇒  10 T=570
⇒T=57 °C

subject: Physics
q:
A wire of length 10 cm and diameter 0.5 mm is used in a bulb. The temperature of the wire is $1727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and power radiated by the wire is 94.2 W. Its emissivity is $\frac{x}{8}$ where $x=$_______
(Given $\sigma=6.0 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~m}^{-2} \mathrm{~K}^{-4}, \pi=3.14$ and assume that the emissivity of wire material is same at all wavelength.)
question:
A wire of length 10 cm and diameter 0.5 mm is used in a bulb. The temperature of the wire is $1727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and power radiated by the wire is 94.2 W. Its emissivity is $\frac{x}{8}$ where $x=$_______
(Given $\sigma=6.0 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~m}^{-2} \mathrm{~K}^{-4}, \pi=3.14$ and assume that the emissivity of wire material is same at all wavelength.)
solution:
$\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{~d}=0.5 \mathrm{~mm}, \mathrm{~T}=1727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=2000 \mathrm{~K}$
Power, $\mathrm{P}=94.2 \mathrm{~W}$
$\mathrm{P}=\varepsilon \sigma \mathrm{AT}^4$
$94.2=\varepsilon \times\left(6 \times 10^{-8}\right)(\pi \mathrm{dL})(2000)^4$
$94.2=\varepsilon \times\left(6 \times 10^{-8}\right)(3.14)(0.5)\left(10^{-3}\right)$
$\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)(2000)^4$
$\varepsilon=\frac{94.2}{(94.2)(16)}=\frac{5}{8}$

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of two outer surfaces of a composite slab, consisting of two materials having coefficients of thermal conductivity $K$ and $2 K$ and thickness $x$ and $4 x$, respectively are $T_2$ and $T_1\left(T_2>T_1\right)$. The rate of heat transfer through the slab, in a steady state is $\left(\frac{A\left(T_2-T_1\right) K}{x}\right) f$, with $f$ equal to
question: The temperature of two outer surfaces of a composite slab, consisting of two materials having coefficients of thermal conductivity $K$ and $2 K$ and thickness $x$ and $4 x$, respectively are $T_2$ and $T_1\left(T_2>T_1\right)$. The rate of heat transfer through the slab, in a steady state is $\left(\frac{A\left(T_2-T_1\right) K}{x}\right) f$, with $f$ equal to
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{q}=\frac{\mathrm{kA}}{\mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{T}_2-\frac{2 \mathrm{~T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1}{3}\right]$
$=\frac{\mathrm{kA}}{3 \mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\right]$

subject: Physics
q: The figure shows a system of two concentric spheres of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ and kept at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively. The radial rate of flow of heat in a substance between the two concentric sphere is proportional to
question: The figure shows a system of two concentric spheres of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ and kept at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively. The radial rate of flow of heat in a substance between the two concentric sphere is proportional to
solution: $\left(\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=\left(\mathrm{T}_1-\mathrm{T}_2\right) \frac{4 \pi \mathrm{r}_1 \mathrm{r}_2 \mathrm{~K}}{\left(\mathrm{r}_2-\mathrm{r}_1\right)}$

subject: Physics
q:
The pressure that has to be applied to the ends of a steel wire of length 10 cm to keep its length constant when its temperature is raised by 100 °C is :
(For steel, Young's modulus is 2×1011 N m-2 and coefficient of thermal expansion is 1.1×10-5 K-1)
question:
The pressure that has to be applied to the ends of a steel wire of length 10 cm to keep its length constant when its temperature is raised by 100 °C is :
(For steel, Young's modulus is 2×1011 N m-2 and coefficient of thermal expansion is 1.1×10-5 K-1)
solution:
Young's modulus, Y=FALΔL

∴FA=YΔLL=YαΔT

∴P=2×​ 1011×1.1×10-5×102

∴ P=2.2×108 Pa

subject: Physics
q: At room temperature $\left(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$, the resistance of a heating element is $50 \Omega$. The temperature coefficient of the material is $2.4 \times 10^{-4}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$. The temperature of the element, when its resistance is $62 \Omega$, is _____ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
question: At room temperature $\left(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$, the resistance of a heating element is $50 \Omega$. The temperature coefficient of the material is $2.4 \times 10^{-4}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$. The temperature of the element, when its resistance is $62 \Omega$, is _____ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_0(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}) \\ & 62=50\left[1+2.4 \times 10^{-4} \Delta \mathrm{T}\right] \\ & \Delta \mathrm{T}=1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}-27^{\circ}=1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & \mathrm{T}=1027^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The thermistors are usually made of
question: The thermistors are usually made of
options: metals with low temperature coefficient of resistivity
metals with high temperature coefficient of resistivity
metal oxides with high temperature coefficient of resistivity "
semiconducting materials having low temperature coefficient of resistivity.
solution:
These are devices whose resistance varies quite markedly with temperature mean having high-temperature coefficient of resistivity. [Their name are derived from thermal resistors]. Depending on their composition they can have either negative temperature coefficient or positive temperature coefficient or positive temperature coefficient or positive temperature coefficient characteristics.
The negative temperature coefficient type consists of a mixture of oxides of iron, nickel, and cobalt with small amounts of other substances. The positive temperature coefficient types are based on barium titanate.

subject: Physics
q: Three rods of identical cross-section and length are made of three different materials of thermal conductivity K1, K2 and K3, respectively. They are joined together at their ends to make a long rod (see figure). One end of the long rod is maintained at 100°C and the other at 0°C (see figure). If the joints of the rod are at 70°C and 20°C in steady and there is no loss of energy from the surface of the rod, the correct relationship between K1, K2 and K3 is :
question: Three rods of identical cross-section and length are made of three different materials of thermal conductivity K1, K2 and K3, respectively. They are joined together at their ends to make a long rod (see figure). One end of the long rod is maintained at 100°C and the other at 0°C (see figure). If the joints of the rod are at 70°C and 20°C in steady and there is no loss of energy from the surface of the rod, the correct relationship between K1, K2 and K3 is :
solution:
K1(100-70)=K2(70-20)=K3(20-0)

K130=K2(50)=K3(20)

⇒K1:K2:K3=10:6:15

K1:K3=2:3

subject: Physics
q:
Due to cold weather, a 1 m water pipe of cross-sectional area 1 cm2 is filled with ice at -10 °C. Resistive heating is used to melt the ice. Current of 0.5 A is passed through 4 kΩ resistance. Assuming that all the heat produced is used for melting, what is the minimum time required?

(Given latent heat of fusion for water/ice=3.33×105 J kg-1, specific heat of ice =2×103 J kg-1 °C-1 and density of ice =103 kg m-3 )
question:
Due to cold weather, a 1 m water pipe of cross-sectional area 1 cm2 is filled with ice at -10 °C. Resistive heating is used to melt the ice. Current of 0.5 A is passed through 4 kΩ resistance. Assuming that all the heat produced is used for melting, what is the minimum time required?

(Given latent heat of fusion for water/ice=3.33×105 J kg-1, specific heat of ice =2×103 J kg-1 °C-1 and density of ice =103 kg m-3 )
solution:
The mass of ice, m=ρAL=103×10-4×1=10-1 kg, 

Energy required to melt the ice, =mSΔT+mL, 

⇒i2Rt=mSΔT+mL
⇒(1)2×103×t=103×1×10-4×2000×10+103×1×10-4×330×103⇒(1)2×103×t=3.53×104⇒t=35.3 s

subject: Physics
q: An ice cube of dimensions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in an insulation box of wall thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The box keeping the ice cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of temperature is brought to a room of temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The rate of melting of ice is approximately: (Latent heat of fusion of ice is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and thermal conducting of insulation wall is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: An ice cube of dimensions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in an insulation box of wall thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The box keeping the ice cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of temperature is brought to a room of temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The rate of melting of ice is approximately: (Latent heat of fusion of ice is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and thermal conducting of insulation wall is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Using the equation of conduction,

dQdt=KAΔTl

Now the total area of the box will be,

A=20.6×0.5+0.5×0.2+0.2×0.6

A=20.3+0.1+0.12

A=20.52=1.04 m2

Therefore,

dQdt=KAΔTl=0.05×1.04×400.01=208 J s-1

This heat will melt the ice. Therefore,

dQdt=dmdtL⇒208=dmdt×3.4×105⇒dmdt=61×10-5 kg s-1

subject: Physics
q: A block of ice at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is slowly heated and converted to steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following curves represent the phenomenon qualitatively:
question: A block of ice at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is slowly heated and converted to steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following curves represent the phenomenon qualitatively:
solution:
When the state of the substance is not changing, temperature of the substance increases when heat is supplied to it. But when the substance changes the state, the heat supplied to it used as latent heat and the temperature remains constant until all the substance converts to other state.

Therefore, temperature of ice first increases from  -10 °C to 0 °C. Then, remains constant at 0°C till whole ice is melted. Then, it increases from 0 °C to 100 °C. Again it remains constant at 100 °C till whole water converts into steam.

subject: Physics
q: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> gram of ice at <math><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> (specific heat <math><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">℃</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) is added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> gram of water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> finally no ice is left and the water is at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn><mi> </mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The value of latent heat of ice, in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
question: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> gram of ice at <math><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> (specific heat <math><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">℃</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) is added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> gram of water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> finally no ice is left and the water is at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn><mi> </mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The value of latent heat of ice, in <math><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
solution:
Principle of calorimetry
M1Sice0--10+M1Lf=M2Swater50-0
M10.510+M1Lf=M250
5M1+M1Lf=M250
Lf=50M2M1-5

subject: Physics
q: A wooden wheel of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is made of two semicircular parts (see figure); The two parts are held together by a ring made of a metal strip of cross sectional area S and length $L$. $L$ is slightly less than $2 \pi R$. To fit the ring on the wheel, it is heated so that its temperature rises by $\Delta \mathrm{T}$ and it just steps over the wheel. As it cools down to surrounding temperature, it presses the semicircular parts together. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is $\alpha$, and its Youngs' modulus is $Y$, the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part is :
question: A wooden wheel of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is made of two semicircular parts (see figure); The two parts are held together by a ring made of a metal strip of cross sectional area S and length $L$. $L$ is slightly less than $2 \pi R$. To fit the ring on the wheel, it is heated so that its temperature rises by $\Delta \mathrm{T}$ and it just steps over the wheel. As it cools down to surrounding temperature, it presses the semicircular parts together. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is $\alpha$, and its Youngs' modulus is $Y$, the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part is :
solution: If temperature increases by $\Delta \mathrm{T}$, Increase in length $\mathrm{L}, \Delta \mathrm{L}=\mathrm{L} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ Let tension developed in the ring is $\mathrm{T}$. $\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{S}}=\mathrm{Y} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{Y} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Y} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ From FBD of one part of the wheel, $\mathrm{F}=2 \mathrm{T}$ Where, $F$ is the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part. $\therefore \mathrm{F}=2 \mathrm{SY} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$

subject: Physics
q: The thermo emf of a thermocouple varies with the temperature $\theta$ of the hot junction as $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{a} \theta+\mathrm{b} \theta^2$ in volts where the ratio $\mathrm{a} / \mathrm{b}$ is $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the cold junction is kept at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the neutral temperature is
question: The thermo emf of a thermocouple varies with the temperature $\theta$ of the hot junction as $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{a} \theta+\mathrm{b} \theta^2$ in volts where the ratio $\mathrm{a} / \mathrm{b}$ is $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the cold junction is kept at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the neutral temperature is
options: no neutral temperature is possible for this thermocouple.
solution:
$E=a \theta+b \theta^2$
At neutral temperature $\mathrm{dE} / \mathrm{d} \theta=0$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b} \theta_{\mathrm{n}}=0 ; \theta_{\mathrm{n}}=-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{~b}}$
Now $\frac{a}{b}=700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (given)
$\theta_n=-700 / 2=-350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Now $\theta_c=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
So, $\theta_n>0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
But mathematically $\theta_{\mathrm{n}} < 0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

subject: Physics
q: $500 \mathrm{~g}$ of water and $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are in a calorimeter whose water equivalent is $40 \mathrm{~g} .10 \mathrm{~g}$ of steam at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is added to it. Then water in the calorimeter is : (Latent heat of ice $=80 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}$, Latent heat of steam $=540 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}$ )
question: $500 \mathrm{~g}$ of water and $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are in a calorimeter whose water equivalent is $40 \mathrm{~g} .10 \mathrm{~g}$ of steam at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is added to it. Then water in the calorimeter is : (Latent heat of ice $=80 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}$, Latent heat of steam $=540 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}$ )
solution:
As $1 \mathrm{~g}$ of steam at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ melts $8 \mathrm{~g}$ of ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
$10 \mathrm{~g}$ of steam will melt $8 \times 10 \mathrm{~g}$ of ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Water in calorimeter $=500+80+10 \mathrm{~g}=590 \mathrm{~g}$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is more closed to a black body?
question: Which of the following is more closed to a black body?
solution: Black body also emits radiation whereas nothing escapes a black hole.

subject: Physics
q: Two different metal bodies A and B of equal mass are heated at a uniform rate under similar conditions. The variation of temperature of the bodies is graphically represented as shown in the figure. The ratio of specific heat capacities is:
question: Two different metal bodies A and B of equal mass are heated at a uniform rate under similar conditions. The variation of temperature of the bodies is graphically represented as shown in the figure. The ratio of specific heat capacities is:
solution:
ΔQΔtA=ΔQΔtB

mSAΔTΔtA=mSBΔTΔtB

SASB=ΔTΔtAΔTΔtB=90/6120/3=1540=38

subject: Physics
q: A body cools from 80oC to 60o in 5 minutes. The temperature for the surrounding is 20oC. The time it takes to cool from 60oC to 40oC is
question: A body cools from 80oC to 60o in 5 minutes. The temperature for the surrounding is 20oC. The time it takes to cool from 60oC to 40oC is
solution:
The formula to calculate the rate of cooling of the object is given by

dTdt=KTf+Ti2-T0   ...1

For the first case, it can be written, using equation (1) that

80-605=K80+602-204=50K   ...2

If t is the required time for the second case, from equation (1), it can be written that

60-40t=K60+402-2020t=30K   ...3

Divide equation (2) by equation (3) and solve to calculate the required time.

420t=50K30K⇒t5=53⇒t=253 min=500 s
